statement: Free allocation of EU ETS allowances to aircraft operators is being phased out under the Fit for 55 reforms adopted in 2023. Under the revised Directive 2003/87/EC, free allocation to aviation is reduced by 25% in 2024, by 50% in 2025, and eliminated entirely from 2026 onwards for EU/EEA operators generally. Correspondingly, the auctioning share increases. From 2024, the aviation cap is also reduced annually by applying a linear reduction factor increased from 2.2% to 4.3% for 2024-2027 (and 4.4% from 2028). The description of free allowances as 'subsidising' airline emissions, while not technically precise (allowances are not a direct subsidy but a regulatory allocation), reflects the economic reality that airlines receiving free allowances avoid purchasing them at market price, reducing their cost of compliance. At ETS carbon prices of EUR 50-80 per tonne (typical 2023-2025 range), free allocation represents a substantial implicit benefit. The phase-out of free allocation is intended to ensure that the aviation sector bears its full carbon cost, in line with the polluter-pays principle.
source_name: European Commission — Allocation to the aviation sector; Directive (EU) 2023/958
caveats: The phase-out schedule applies to the general EU/EEA framework. Iceland negotiated a specific exemption (see ETS-DATA-002). The characterisation of free allowances as a 'subsidy' is debated — the European Commission considers free allocation a transitional measure to address carbon leakage risk, not a subsidy in the state-aid sense. Airlines argue that free allocation compensates for competitive distortion with non-ETS carriers on international routes.

related_entries.statement: International aviation emissions within the European Economic Area fall under the EU Emissions Trading System. Aviation was included in the EU ETS by Directive 2008/101/EC of 19 November 2008, amending Directive 2003/87/EC. The original 2008 legislation was designed to cover CO2 emissions from all flights departing from, arriving at, or within the EEA. However, due to international opposition, the scope was temporarily reduced by Regulation (EU) No 421/2014 to cover only intra-EEA flights (the 'reduced scope' or 'stop the clock' derogation), and this restriction has been repeatedly extended. Following the 2023 revision (Directive (EU) 2023/958), the EU ETS for aviation continues to apply to all intra-EEA flights. The limitation to intra-EEA scope was extended one final time until the start of 2027; by July 2026, the European Commission will assess whether to expand coverage to flights departing the EEA to third countries not participating in ICAO's CORSIA scheme. The aviation ETS cap was initially set at 95% of 2004-2006 average emissions (approximately 221.4 million allowances). Aircraft operators were originally allocated 82% of allowances for free, with 15% auctioned and 3% held as a special reserve for new entrants.

Iceland negotiated a special arrangement to continue receiving free EU ETS aviation allowances beyond the standard phase-out schedule. In May 2023, at the Council of Europe summit in Reykjavik, Iceland and the European Commission reached a preliminary agreement for Icelandic aircraft operators to continue receiving free EU ETS emissions allowances up to and including the year 2026. This is one year beyond the general EU/EEA phase-out (where free allocation drops to zero from 2026). The Icelandic government argued that the standard rules harm the competitive position of Icelandic airlines and fail to account for Iceland's geographical isolation, which makes its citizens more dependent on air transport than other EEA residents. The EU sought to grant the Icelandic aviation sector an extra year of free allowances as a special solution compatible with the EU's broader climate goals. The agreement was subject to approval by EU Member States, the Icelandic government, and the Icelandic parliament (Althingi). Claims that the exemption extends to 'end of 2027' appear to conflate two different things: Iceland's free allocation exemption (through 2026) and the broader EU ETS aviation geographical scope limitation to intra-EEA flights (extended until start of 2027).
